settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

# Smart Blind ESP32 Firmware

This firmware is now aligned to the actual BOM you shared for the smart blind capstone.

## Target hardware

- ESP32 DevKitC WROOM-32D V4 CH9102X
- 2x 18650 cells
- TP4056 USB-C charger
- XL6009 step-up converter
- 5V 400mA solar panel
- BH1750 light sensor
- rain sensor module
- 1.3 inch OLED module
- BME680 environmental sensor
- 2x 8-pixel WS2812B stick modules
- lift motor:
  - hybrid stepper motor
  - DRV8825
  - 2x KW-10R limit switch
- slat tilt motor:
  - 5V stepper motor
  - ULN2003 board

## What the firmware does

- Reads ambient light from `BH1750`.
- Reads temperature, humidity, pressure, and gas resistance from `BME680`.
- Calculates an approximate indoor air score from `BME680` humidity and gas resistance.
- Reads the rain sensor digital output.
- Reads battery voltage through an analog divider.
- Controls the lift axis through `DRV8825`.
- Homes the lift axis with top and bottom `KW-10R` limit switches.
- Controls the slat tilt axis with the `ULN2003` stepper.
- Drives `16` total WS2812 LEDs.
- Shows live status on the OLED.
- Exposes a small built-in web UI and JSON API over Wi-Fi.

## Important hardware notes

- `TP4056` is a `1S` charger. That means your two `18650` cells must be used as `1S2P`, not `2S`.
- `BME680` does not measure true `CO2`, `eCO2`, or `PM2.5`. In this firmware it is used as an approximate indoor air-quality sensor only.
- Your current BOM does not include a real outdoor dust sensor, so the presentation's outdoor fine-dust feature is not implemented here.
- Many `1.3"` OLED modules are `SH1106`, so this firmware defaults to `SH1106`. If your display is actually `SSD1306`, change `APP_USE_SH1106_DISPLAY` in [include/app_config.h](include/app_config.h).

## Project files

- [platformio.ini](platformio.ini): PlatformIO environment and libraries
- [include/app_config.h](include/app_config.h): pin map, thresholds, addresses, schedules
- [src/main.cpp](src/main.cpp): main firmware logic

## Default pin map

- `GPIO21`: I2C SDA
- `GPIO22`: I2C SCL
- `GPIO35`: rain sensor digital input
- `GPIO36`: battery voltage sense
- `GPIO25`: passive buzzer
- `GPIO26`: WS2812 data
- `GPIO27`: DRV8825 STEP
- `GPIO14`: DRV8825 DIR
- `GPIO13`: DRV8825 ENABLE
- `GPIO32`: lift bottom limit switch
- `GPIO33`: lift top limit switch
- `GPIO16`: ULN2003 IN1
- `GPIO17`: ULN2003 IN2
- `GPIO18`: ULN2003 IN3
- `GPIO19`: ULN2003 IN4

## Implemented behavior

- `auto sunlight` mode:
  - very bright outdoor light: mostly close blind
  - normal daylight: open blind for daylight
  - dark / night: close blind for privacy
  - rain detected: close blind
- `manual` mode:
  - set lift and tilt percentages from the web UI or API
- wake / sleep mood light:
  - wake mode fades from orange to white
  - sleep mode fades down to off
- lift homing:
  - startup homing runs automatically
  - `/api/home` can re-home the lift axis later

## Web API

- `GET /api/status`
- `POST /api/mode?mode=auto|manual`
- `POST /api/blinds?lift=0..100&tilt=0..100`
- `POST /api/light?mode=warm_white|soft_blue|romantic_purple|vital_green|soft_orange|off`
- `POST /api/schedule?wake=07:00&sleep=23:30&enableWake=1&enableSleep=1`
- `POST /api/home`

The root page `/` gives you a simple browser control page for testing before the mobile app exists.

## Bring-up steps

1. Open the project in VS Code with PlatformIO.
2. Edit Wi-Fi credentials in [include/app_config.h](include/app_config.h).
3. Check sensor I2C addresses:
   - `BH1750` usually `0x23`
   - `BME680` often `0x76` or `0x77`
4. Verify OLED controller type and switch the display macro if needed.
5. Adjust `APP_BATTERY_DIVIDER_RATIO` to match your resistor divider.
6. Adjust `APP_LIFT_FALLBACK_TRAVEL_STEPS` and `APP_TILT_MAX_STEPS` after first mechanical test.
7. Upload firmware and open serial monitor at `115200`.
8. If station Wi-Fi is not configured, connect to the ESP32 access point named `smart-blind`.

## What you may still want to add

- real outdoor dust sensing
- real `CO2` or `eCO2` sensing
- mobile app integration
- MQTT or Home Assistant integration
- calibration storage for the BME680 air score
- safer power-path and battery telemetry if you move beyond prototype level

content.text:
`Arduino Nano ESP32` кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң кё°ліё нҸ¬нҢ…мқҖ н•ҙл’ҖмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ. л№Ңл“ң нғҖк№ғмқ„ `arduino_nano_esp32`лЎң л°”кҫёкі , DevKitC м „мҡ© GPIO лҢҖмӢ  Nano ESP32м—җм„ң м“°кё° мү¬мҡҙ `SDA/SCL`, `D2-D12`, `A0-A1` кё°л°ҳ н•Җл§өмңјлЎң мҳ®кІјмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

ліҖкІҪн•ң н•өмӢ¬ нҢҢмқјмқҖ [platformio.ini](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/platformio.ini), [include/app_config.h](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/include/app_config.h), [README.md](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/README.md)мһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. нҠ№нһҲ `platformio.ini`лҠ” Nano ESP32 ліҙл“ңлЎң л°”лҖҢм—Ҳкі , [include/app_config.h](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/include/app_config.h)м—җм„ңлҠ” нҳ„мһ¬ кё°ліё л§Өн•‘мқҙ м•„лһҳмІҳлҹј мһЎнҳҖ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

- `SDA/SCL`: I2C
- `D2`: rain sensor
- `A0`: battery sense
- `D3`: buzzer
- `D4`: WS2812
- `D5/D6/D7`: DRV8825
- `D8/D9`: limit switches
- `D10/D11/D12/A1`: ULN2003

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message: м•„л‘җмқҙл…ё sda scl мң„м№ҳк°Җ м–ҙл””м•ј

`Arduino Nano ESP32`м—җм„ң `SDA`, `SCL`мқҖ ліҙл“ңм—җ к·ёлҢҖлЎң `SDA`, `SCL`мқҙлқјкі  мқёмҮ„лҗң I2C м „мҡ© н•Җмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

ліҙнҶө Nano кі„м—ҙ ліҙл“ңм—җм„ңлҠ”:
- `SDA`: м•„лӮ лЎңк·ё н•Җ мӨ„ мӘҪ лҒқл¶Җл¶„ к·јмІҳ
- `SCL`: `SDA` л°”лЎң мҳҶ

мҰү, мқҙ ліҙл“ңлҠ” көімқҙ ESP32мқҳ лӮҙл¶Җ GPIO лІҲнҳёлҘј мҷёмҡё н•„мҡ” м—Ҷмқҙ, ліҙл“ң мӢӨнҒ¬ мқёмҮ„лҗң `SDA`, `SCL` н•Җм—җ к·ёлҢҖлЎң м—°кІ°н•ҳл©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.

м—°кІ°мқҖ мқҙл ҮкІҢ ліҙл©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
- BH1750 `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- BH1750 `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`
- BME680 `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- BME680 `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`
- OLED `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- OLED `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ м ңк°Җ л°”лЎң `Arduino Nano ESP32` кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң м „мІҙ л°°м„ лҸ„лҘј н…ҚмҠӨнҠё н‘ңлЎң л§Ңл“Өм–ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

`Arduino Nano ESP32`м—җм„ң `SDA`, `SCL`мқҖ ліҙл“ңм—җ к·ёлҢҖлЎң `SDA`, `SCL`мқҙлқјкі  мқёмҮ„лҗң I2C м „мҡ© н•Җмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

ліҙнҶө Nano кі„м—ҙ ліҙл“ңм—җм„ңлҠ”:
- `SDA`: м•„лӮ лЎңк·ё н•Җ мӨ„ мӘҪ лҒқл¶Җл¶„ к·јмІҳ
- `SCL`: `SDA` л°”лЎң мҳҶ

мҰү, мқҙ ліҙл“ңлҠ” көімқҙ ESP32мқҳ лӮҙл¶Җ GPIO лІҲнҳёлҘј мҷёмҡё н•„мҡ” м—Ҷмқҙ, ліҙл“ң мӢӨнҒ¬ мқёмҮ„лҗң `SDA`, `SCL` н•Җм—җ к·ёлҢҖлЎң м—°кІ°н•ҳл©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.

м—°кІ°мқҖ мқҙл ҮкІҢ ліҙл©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
- BH1750 `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- BH1750 `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`
- BME680 `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- BME680 `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`
- OLED `SDA` -> Nano ESP32 `SDA`
- OLED `SCL` -> Nano ESP32 `SCL`

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ м ңк°Җ л°”лЎң `Arduino Nano ESP32` кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң м „мІҙ л°°м„ лҸ„лҘј н…ҚмҠӨнҠё н‘ңлЎң л§Ңл“Өм–ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: vbusк°Җ I2C мқёк°Җ

payload.message: vbusк°Җ I2C мқёк°Җ

м•„лӢҲмҡ”. `VBUS`лҠ” I2Cк°Җ м•„лӢҷлӢҲлӢӨ.

мқҳлҜёк°Җ мҷ„м „нһҲ лӢӨлҰ…лӢҲлӢӨ.
- `VBUS`: USB м „мӣҗм„ , ліҙнҶө `5V`
- `SDA`: I2C лҚ°мқҙн„°м„ 
- `SCL`: I2C нҒҙлҹӯм„ 

мҰү `VBUS`лҠ” м „мӣҗмқҙкі , `SDA/SCL`мқҖ нҶөмӢ  н•Җмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлһҳм„ң м„јм„ңлӮҳ OLEDмқҳ I2C м—°кІ°мқҖ `VBUS`м—җ н•ҳл©ҙ м•Ҳ лҗҳкі , мқҙл ҮкІҢ н•ҙм•ј н•©лӢҲлӢӨ.

- м „мӣҗ `VCC` -> ліҙл“ңмқҳ `3.3V` лҳҗлҠ” лӘЁл“Ҳ мҡ”кө¬ м „м••
- м ‘м§Җ `GND` -> `GND`
- лҚ°мқҙн„° `SDA` -> `SDA`
- нҒҙлҹӯ `SCL` -> `SCL`

м •лҰ¬н•ҳл©ҙ `VBUS = м „мӣҗ`, `SDA/SCL = I2C нҶөмӢ `мһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ лӢӨмқҢ лӢөліҖм—җм„ң `Arduino Nano ESP32` н•Җ мқҙлҰ„л“Ө(`VBUS`, `VIN`, `3V3`, `SDA`, `SCL`, `GND`) к°Ғк°Ғмқҙ л¬ҙмҠЁ м—ӯн• мқём§Җ н‘ңлЎң л”ұ м •лҰ¬н•ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

`Arduino Nano ESP32`м—җм„ңлҠ” ліҙл“ңм—җ `SDA`, `SCL`лқјкі  м ҒнһҢ кё°ліё I2C н•Җмқ„ м“ё мҲҳлҸ„ мһҲм§Җл§Ң, ESP32 кі„м—ҙ нҠ№м„ұмғҒ `Wire.begin(SDAн•Җ, SCLн•Җ)` нҳ•нғңлЎң мӣҗн•ҳлҠ” GPIOлҘј м§Җм •н•ҙм„ң м“°лҠ” л°©мӢқлҸ„ к°ҖлҠҘн•©лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлһҳм„ң вҖңI2C м „мҡ© кі м • н•Җл§Ң мһҲлӢӨвҖқкё°ліҙлӢӨлҠ” вҖңкё°ліё н•Җмқҙ мһҲкі , н•„мҡ”н•ҳл©ҙ мҪ”л“ңм—җм„ң мһ¬м§Җм • к°ҖлҠҘн•ҳлӢӨвҖқм—җ к°Җк№қмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

нҳ„мһ¬ мҪ”л“ңлҸ„ к·ё л°©мӢқмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. [src/main.cpp](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/src/main.cpp)м—җм„ң:

```cpp
Wire.begin(APP_I2C_SDA_PIN, APP_I2C_SCL_PIN);
```

к·ёлҰ¬кі  мӢӨм ң к°’мқҖ [include/app_config.h](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/include/app_config.h)м—җм„ң м •н•©лӢҲлӢӨ.

мҰү н•өмӢ¬мқҖ:
- I2CлҠ” лқјмқҙлёҢлҹ¬лҰ¬ `Wire`лҘј мӮ¬мҡ©
- `Wire.begin(sda, scl)`лЎң н•Җ м§Җм • к°ҖлҠҘ
- к·ёлһҳм„ң ліҙл“ң мӢӨнҒ¬мқҳ кё°ліё `SDA/SCL`мқ„ мҚЁлҸ„ лҗҳкі 
- мӣҗн•ҳл©ҙ лӢӨлҘё GPIOлЎң л°”кҝ”лҸ„ лҗЁ

м ңк°Җ м§ҖкёҲ `app_config.h`м—җлҠ” мқҙл ҮкІҢ л„Јм–ҙл‘” мғҒнғңмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

```cpp
static constexpr uint8_t APP_I2C_SDA_PIN = SDA;
static constexpr uint8_t APP_I2C_SCL_PIN = SCL;
```

мқҙкұҙ вҖңліҙл“ң кё°ліё I2C н•Җмқ„ м“°мһҗвҖқлҠ” мқҳлҜёмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. л§Ңм•Ҫ м§Ғм ‘ GPIO лІҲнҳёлЎң кі м •н•ҳкі  мӢ¶мңјл©ҙ мқҙл ҮкІҢ л°”кҝҖ мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

```cpp
static constexpr uint8_t APP_I2C_SDA_PIN = 11;
static constexpr uint8_t APP_I2C_SCL_PIN = 12;
```

лӢЁ, м–ҙл–Ө лІҲнҳёк°Җ мӢӨм ң `Arduino Nano ESP32`м—җм„ң м•Ҳм „н•ңм§ҖлҠ” ліҙл“ң н•Җл§ө кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң нҷ•мқён•ҙм•ј н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. л¬ҙмһ‘м • м•„л¬ҙ н•ҖмқҙлӮҳ м“°л©ҙ л¶ҖнҢ… мҠӨнҠёлһ©мқҙлӮҳ лӢӨлҘё кё°лҠҘкіј м¶©лҸҢн•  мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ лӢӨмқҢмңјлЎң м ңк°Җ н•ҙл“ңлҰҙ мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” кұҙ:
- Nano ESP32м—җм„ң м•Ҳм „н•ң I2Cмҡ© GPIO нӣ„ліҙлҘј кіЁлқјл“ңлҰ¬кё°
- к·ё н•Җ лІҲнҳёлЎң `app_config.h`лҘј л°”лЎң л°”кҝ”л“ңлҰ¬кё°
- м„јм„ң/OLEDк№Ңм§Җ нҸ¬н•Ён•ң мӢӨм ң л°°м„ н‘ңлЎң м •лҰ¬н•ҳкё°

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

- `A5 = SCL`

к·ёлһҳм„ң I2C л°°м„ н•  л•Ң к·ёл ҮкІҢ л§Һмқҙ кё°м–өн•©лӢҲлӢӨ.

лӢӨл§Ң `Arduino Nano ESP32`лҠ” мҷ„м „нһҲ к°ҷмқҖ ліҙл“ңлҠ” м•„лӢҲлқјм„ң, вҖңл¬ҙмЎ°кұҙ A4/A5вҖқлқјкі  лӢЁм •н•ҳкё°ліҙлӢӨ ліҙл“ңмқҳ мӢӨм ң н•Җл§өкіј мҪ”л“ңлҘј к°ҷмқҙ л§һм¶”лҠ” кІҢ м•Ҳм „н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. мҰү AVR NanoмІҳлҹј мҷёмҡ°кё°ліҙлӢӨлҠ”:

- ліҙл“ң кё°ліё I2C н•Җмқ„ м“ём§Җ
- м•„лӢҲл©ҙ `Wire.begin(sda, scl)`лЎң м§Ғм ‘ м§Җм •н• м§Җ

мқҙкұё кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң к°ҖлҠ” кІҢ мўӢмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

м§ҖкёҲ н•ҳл ӨлҠ” мһ‘м—…мқҖ `Nano ESP32`лқјм„ң, м ңк°Җ лӢӨмқҢ лӢЁкі„лЎң м •нҷ•н•ҳкІҢ н•ҙл“ңлҰҙ мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” кұҙ л‘ҳ мӨ‘ н•ҳлӮҳмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

1. `Nano ESP32`мқҳ мӢӨм ң I2C кё°ліё н•Җ нҷ•мқён•ҙм„ң мҪ”л“ң кі м •
2. м•„мҳҲ `A4/A5` к°җк°ҒмІҳлҹј мқҙн•ҙн•ҳкё° мү¬мҡҙ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ м§Җм • н•ҖмңјлЎң мҪ”л“ң мһ¬м„Өм •

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ м ңк°Җ м§ҖкёҲ `Arduino Nano ESP32` кё°мӨҖ мӢӨм ң к¶ҢмһҘ I2C н•Җмқ„ нҷ•мқён•ҙм„ң [include/app_config.h](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/include/app_config.h)м—җ м •нҷ•нһҲ л°ҳмҳҒн•ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

| ULN2003 | `VCC` | мҷёл¶Җ `5V` | 28BYJ-48 кі„м—ҙмқҙл©ҙ ліҙнҶө 5V |
| л°°н„°лҰ¬ м „м•• мёЎм • | л¶„м•• м¶ңл Ҙ | `A0` | л°ҳл“ңмӢң л¶„м•• нӣ„ мһ…л Ҙ |
| л°°н„°лҰ¬ м „м•• мёЎм • | л¶„м•• GND | `GND` | кіөнҶө м ‘м§Җ |

**м „мӣҗ кё°мӨҖ**
- `3V3`: BH1750, BME680, OLED, DRV8825 лЎңм§Ғм „мӣҗмҡ©
- `VBUS` лҳҗлҠ” мҷёл¶Җ `5V`: WS2812B, ULN2003/5V мҠӨн…ҢнҚјмІҳлҹј 5V н•„мҡ”н•ң мһҘм№ҳмҡ©
- `GND`: м „л¶Җ л°ҳл“ңмӢң кіөнҶө м ‘м§Җ

**мӨ‘мҡ”**
- `I2C`лҠ” `A4 = SDA`, `A5 = SCL`лЎң мһЎлҠ” кІҢ Nano кі„м—ҙ к°җк°ҒмғҒ к°ҖмһҘ мқҙн•ҙн•ҳкё° мүҪмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.
- `WS2812B`мҷҖ лӘЁн„°лҘҳлҠ” ліҙл“ңм—җм„ң м§Ғм ‘ лЁ№мқҙм§Җ л§җкі  мҷёл¶Җ м „мӣҗмқ„ м“°лҠ” мӘҪмқҙ м•Ҳм „н•©лӢҲлӢӨ.
- мҷёл¶Җ `5V`лҘј м“°лҚ”лқјлҸ„ `GND`лҠ” Nano ESP32мҷҖ кјӯ кіөнҶөмңјлЎң л¬¶м–ҙм•ј н•©лӢҲлӢӨ.
- ESP32лҠ” `3.3V лЎңм§Ғ`мқҙлқј, I2C лӘЁл“Ҳмқҙ `5V pull-up`мқ„ к°•н•ҳкІҢ кұ°лҠ” нғҖмһ…мқҙл©ҙ мЈјмқҳк°Җ н•„мҡ”н•©лӢҲлӢӨ.

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ лӢӨмқҢмңјлЎң [include/app_config.h](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/include/app_config.h)лҸ„ мқҙ н‘ң кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң `A4/A5` л°ҳмҳҒлҗҳкІҢ л°”лЎң мҲҳм •н•ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

content.text:
л„Ө. `Arduino Nano ESP32` кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң, м§ҖкёҲ н”„лЎңм қнҠёлҘј л°°м„ н•ҳкё° мүҪкІҢ м „мӣҗ нҸ¬н•Ё н‘ңлЎң м •лҰ¬н•ҳл©ҙ м•„лһҳмІҳлҹј м“°л©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.

| лҰ¬лҜёнҠё мҠӨмң„м№ҳ(мғҒлӢЁ) | н•ңмӘҪ | `D9` | лӢӨлҘё мӘҪмқҖ `GND` |

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: мқҙ мҠӨл Ҳл“ң нҸҙлҚ” м „мІҙлҘј м–ҙл–»кІҢ л°ұм—…н•ҙм„ң лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ң ліҙм§Җ

к°ҖмһҘ мү¬мҡҙ л°©лІ•мқҖ мқҙ нҸҙлҚ” мһҗмІҙлҘј к·ёлҢҖлЎң ліөмӮ¬н•ҳкұ°лӮҳ, мқҙлҜё GitHubм—җ мҳ¬лқјк°Җ мһҲмңјлӢҲ лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ң лӢӨмӢң нҒҙлЎ н•ҳлҠ” кІҒлӢҲлӢӨ.

к°ҖмһҘ м¶”мІңн•ҳлҠ” л°©лІ•:
- GitHub л°ұм—…: `zemcan/CAPS`м—җ мқҙлҜё н‘ёмӢңлҸј мһҲмңјлӢҲ к°ҖмһҘ м•Ҳм „н•©лӢҲлӢӨ
- нҸҙлҚ” нҶөм§ё ліөмӮ¬: `C:\Users\KBC\Documents\smart`лҘј лӢӨлҘё л“ңлқјмқҙлёҢлӮҳ USBлЎң ліөмӮ¬
- м••м¶• л°ұм—…: `smart` нҸҙлҚ”лҘј zipмңјлЎң м••м¶•н•ҙм„ң ліҙкҙҖ

лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ң лӢӨмӢң ліҙл Өл©ҙ ліҙнҶө л‘җ к°Җм§Җмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

1. нҸҙлҚ” к·ёлҢҖлЎң ліөмӮ¬н•ң кІҪмҡ°  
ліөмӮ¬н•ң нҸҙлҚ”лҘј к·ёлғҘ м—ҙл©ҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. `.git`к№Ңм§Җ к°ҷмқҙ ліөмӮ¬лҗҳлҜҖлЎң Git мқҙл ҘлҸ„ мң м§Җлҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.

2. GitHubм—җм„ң лӢӨмӢң л°ӣлҠ” кІҪмҡ°  
лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ң:
```powershell
git clone https://github.com/zemcan/CAPS.git
```

к·ёлҹ¬л©ҙ мғҲ нҸҙлҚ”м—җ к°ҷмқҖ н”„лЎңм қнҠёк°Җ лӮҙл ӨмҳөлӢҲлӢӨ.

мЈјмқҳн•  м җ:
- `.git` нҸҙлҚ”к№Ңм§Җ нҸ¬н•Ён•ҙм•ј вҖңк·ёлғҘ нҢҢмқј ліөмӮ¬вҖқк°Җ м•„лӢҲлқј мҷ„м „н•ң м ҖмһҘмҶҢ л°ұм—…мһ…лӢҲлӢӨ
- GitHubм—җ мҳ¬лқјк°Җм§Җ м•ҠмқҖ лЎңм»¬ м „мҡ© нҢҢмқјмқҙ мһҲмңјл©ҙ нҸҙлҚ” ліөмӮ¬к°Җ лҚ” м•Ҳм „н•©лӢҲлӢӨ
- м§ҖкёҲмқҖ мһ‘м—…нҠёлҰ¬к°Җ к№ЁлҒ—н•ҳлӢҲ, GitHub кё°мӨҖмңјлЎңлҸ„ л°ұм—… мғҒнғңк°Җ мўӢмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ м ңк°Җ л°”лЎң
- `smart` нҸҙлҚ”лҘј zipмңјлЎң м••м¶•н•ҙмЈјкұ°лӮҳ
- лӢӨлҘё кІҪлЎңлЎң ліөмӮ¬н•ҙмЈјкұ°лӮҳ
- л°ұм—…мҡ© нҸҙлҚ” кө¬мЎ°к№Ңм§Җ л§Ңл“Өм–ҙл“ңлҰҙкІҢмҡ”.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.message: к№ғл§җкі  лҢҖнҷ” мҠӨл Ҳл“ң

лҢҖнҷ” мҠӨл Ҳл“ң мһҗмІҙлҘј л°ұм—…н•ҙм„ң лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ң ліҙлҠ” кұҙ, нҸҙлҚ” ліөмӮ¬мІҳлҹј лӢЁмҲңн•ҳм§Җ м•ҠмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ. мқҙкұҙ Git м ҖмһҘмҶҢмҷҖ лі„к°ңлЎң Codex м•ұмқҳ лҢҖнҷ” лҚ°мқҙн„°м—җ л“Өм–ҙ мһҲкё° л•Ңл¬ёмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

м •лҰ¬н•ҳл©ҙ:
- н”„лЎңм қнҠё нҢҢмқј л°ұм—…: нҸҙлҚ” ліөмӮ¬лӮҳ GitмңјлЎң к°ҖлҠҘ
- лҢҖнҷ” мҠӨл Ҳл“ң л°ұм—…: м•ұмқҙ л”°лЎң м ҖмһҘн•ҳлҠ” лҢҖнҷ” кё°лЎқ мҳҒм—ӯмқҙ н•„мҡ”

м ңк°Җ м§ҖкёҲ л°”лЎң нҷ•мӢӨнһҲ л§җн•  мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” кұҙ:
- нҳ„мһ¬ мӣҢнҒ¬мҠӨнҺҳмқҙмҠӨ нҸҙлҚ” `C:\Users\KBC\Documents\smart` м•Ҳм—җлҠ” лҢҖнҷ” мҠӨл Ҳл“ң ліёл¬ёмқҙ мһҗлҸҷмңјлЎң к°ҷмқҙ м ҖмһҘлҗҳлҠ” кө¬мЎ°лҠ” м•„лӢҷлӢҲлӢӨ
- к·ёлһҳм„ң нҸҙлҚ”л§Ң ліөмӮ¬н•ҙлҸ„ вҖңлҢҖнҷ”вҖқк№Ңм§Җ к°ҷмқҙ мқҙлҸҷлҗҳм§ҖлҠ” м•ҠмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ

нҳ„мӢӨм Ғмқё л°©лІ•мқҖ ліҙнҶө мқҙ м…Ӣмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.
- лҢҖнҷ” лӮҙмҡ©мқ„ ліөмӮ¬н•ҙм„ң `README`лӮҳ `notes.md` к°ҷмқҖ нҢҢмқјлЎң м ҖмһҘ
- мҠӨнҒ¬лҰ°мғ·/PDFлЎң ліҙкҙҖ
- м•ұм—җм„ң лҢҖнҷ” лӮҙліҙлӮҙкё° кё°лҠҘмқҙ мһҲмңјл©ҙ к·ёкұё мӮ¬мҡ©

мӣҗн•ҳмӢңл©ҙ м ңк°Җ м§ҖкёҲ мқҙ лҢҖнҷ”мқҳ н•өмӢ¬ лӮҙмҡ©мқ„ м •лҰ¬н•ҙм„ң
[C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/docs/thread-notes.md](C:/Users/KBC/Documents/smart/docs/thread-notes.md)
к°ҷмқҖ нҢҢмқјлЎң л§Ңл“Өм–ҙл“ңлҰҙ мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.  
к·ёл ҮкІҢ н•ҳл©ҙ лӢӨлҘё мң„м№ҳм—җм„ңлҸ„ н”„лЎңм қнҠёмҷҖ н•Ёк»ҳ ліј мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.
